one_line_takeaway: By porting randomized smoothing to a two-network (UPV-style) LLM watermark detector -- with Gaussian noise over word embeddings and Uniform noise over a novel token-permutation space -- the paper delivers the first provable certified-robustness guarantee for text watermarks, matching or beating Unigram/SIR on paraphrase attacks at the cost of uncontrollable FPR and weakness on short texts.
key_finding: The method attains certified robustness while staying competitive on empirical F1. About 80% of certified radii on the embedding space exceed 20.0; with average token embedding l2-norm 2.5 and average pairwise l2-distance 3.2, a radius of 20.0 is interpreted as tolerating roughly 44 random token replacements on average. On empirical paraphrasing (Table 3) it often beats both baselines -- e.g. DIPPER-1 on OPT (sampling) F1 0.898 for Ours vs 0.751 Unigram / 0.805 SIR, and GPT-3.5 rewriting on GPT-2 (sampling) F1 0.956 vs 0.922 / 0.808 -- and is notably more stable across LLMs, where SIR and Unigram degrade sharply on OPT-1.3B. Adding permutation (Uniform) noise measurably helps under order-shuffling paraphrase: DIPPER-2 sampling improves TPR 0.688->0.764 and F1 0.808->0.851 (Table 2). The method also yields lower perplexity than both baselines.
core_contribution: The paper proposes what it claims is the first certified-robust watermarking algorithm for LLM-generated text, built on randomized smoothing. Following the UPV framework (two neural networks with a shared embedding layer: a watermark generator G and a binary-classifier detector D), the authors' own contribution is: (1) modeling watermark-removal attacks as perturbations in two separate spaces -- an embedding/word space (content changes) and a newly introduced permutation space (token-order changes) -- and injecting Gaussian noise into the embedding space and Uniform noise into the permutation space during detector training and inference; (2) deriving certified radii for both spaces (embedding radius RADe = sigma*Phi^-1(pA); permutation radius RADp = lambda*(pA-pB)), giving a provable guarantee that the detector's decision is invariant under any perturbation within those radii; (3) practical training changes over UPV: training the detector on real LLM-generated (GPT-2) watermarked text instead of UPV's randomly-generated dataset to match the real z-score/word-frequency distribution, and using a third-party GloVe tokenizer plus a length-preserving 'encode trick' so one detector generalizes across LLMs with different tokenizers. Note: the smoothing theorems themselves are not new -- the proofs are explicitly attributed to Cohen et al. [9] and Text-CRS [55]; the contribution is transferring/adapting them to watermarking.
threat_model: Adversary is a user who possesses watermarked AI-generated text and wants to strip the watermark so the text evades detection (watermark-removal attacks). Attacks considered: word-level edits (synonym replacement, deletion, word reordering/swapping), sentence-level paraphrasing (DIPPER at two lexical/order-diversity settings, and ChatGPT gpt-3.5-turbo rewriting), plus the emoji attack (perturbing preceding-token hashes) and copy-paste dilution (inserting 150 or 200 watermarked tokens among 600 human tokens). The distinctive goal versus prior robust watermarks is a *provable* guarantee that holds against unseen/adaptive attacks, not just the specific empirical attacks tested -- any perturbation whose embedding/permutation displacement stays within the certified radius cannot flip the detector's verdict. The paper also notes (in related work, not as its own defense) spoofing/forgery attacks but does not target them.
limitations: Acknowledged/evident in the paper's own text: (1) the detector's FPR cannot be pinned to a fixed target (unlike SIR/Unigram's tunable z-score threshold), complicating operating-point control; (2) in the no-attack setting it is slightly worse than baselines (e.g. GPT-2 sampling F1 0.991 vs 0.994), attributed to randomized smoothing's inherent cost; (3) under low-cost editing attacks it underperforms Unigram on deletion/swapping and on synonym replacement (only comparable to SIR there, Fig. 6); (4) detection quality collapses for short texts (F1 far below ideal at 50 tokens, stabilizing only past ~150 tokens); (5) a fixed detector input-length limit forced a sliding-window workaround for copy-paste dilution, and results were reported only for a hand-picked worst-case window rather than end-to-end; (6) the certified radius in permutation space is hard to interpret intuitively; (7) the certification theorems are inherited from Cohen et al. and Text-CRS rather than newly proven; (8) certified guarantees are probabilistic (hold with confidence alpha via the binomial test), and evaluation is limited to models up to LLaMA-7B.

datasets_used: dbpedia (subset; non-watermarked training texts and prompts)
C4 (subset; test set of 500 watermarked + 500 non-watermarked)
Training set: 5000 GPT-2-generated watermarked + 5000 non-watermarked dbpedia texts
GloVe-6B (as third-party tokenizer/embeddings, not a corpus)
quantitative_results: Average token embedding l2-norm = 2.5; average pairwise l2-distance = 3.2; radius 20.0 ~= tolerating ~44 random token replacements
No-attack GPT-2 sampling F1: Ours 0.991 vs Unigram 0.994, SIR 0.992
DIPPER-1 OPT sampling F1: Ours 0.898 vs Unigram 0.751, SIR 0.805
DIPPER-2 OPT beam-search F1: Ours 0.934 vs Unigram 0.890, SIR 0.884
GPT-3.5 rewrite GPT-2 sampling F1: Ours 0.956 vs Unigram 0.922, SIR 0.808
Permutation-noise ablation (Table 2), DIPPER-2 sampling: TPR 0.688 (w/o) -> 0.764 (w/ noise), F1 0.808 -> 0.851
Emoji attack F1: Ours 0.929 (FPR 0.002) vs Unigram 0.936, SIR 0.856
Copy-Paste(200) F1: Ours 0.960 vs Unigram 0.979, SIR 0.949
Detection F1 unstable at 50 tokens, stabilizes beyond ~150 tokens
baselines_compared: Unigram [58] (Zhao et al., fixed green-token list, KGW variant)
SIR [31] (Liu et al., semantic-invariant robust watermark)
